participants.extracted: Nineteen adults with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis were enrolled from a single center in southern Switzerland between 2010 and 2011. Patients were 18 to 60 years old and had been receiving natalizumab for at least 12 months because of fear of or significant risk for progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy; they also had to be clinically stable on natalizumab, with no relapses or disability progression for at least 6 months and no gadolinium-enhancing lesions on baseline MRI. Sixteen of the 19 randomized participants completed the study (10 assigned to continue natalizumab and 9 assigned to switch to interferon beta-1b), with one withdrawal from each arm and one interferon beta-1b participant switching to rescue glatiramer acetate. Baseline characteristics were similar between groups.

interventions.extracted: Participants were randomized 1:1 to continue natalizumab 300 mg given intravenously every month or to switch (“de-escalate”) to interferon beta-1b 250 µg administered subcutaneously every other day, started within 30 days after the last natalizumab infusion. Patients who could not tolerate interferon beta-1b were permitted to switch to rescue therapy with glatiramer acetate 20 mg subcutaneously once daily.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: time to first on-study relapse after randomization, assessed over the 1-year follow-up. Secondary outcomes: number of relapses over 1 year; proportion of relapse-free patients over 1 year; severity of relapses measured by change in EDSS score (severe relapse defined as an increase of at least 1.5 EDSS points); 3-month confirmed disability progression measured by EDSS increase of at least 1.0 point; number of new T2-hyperintense lesions on MRI at months 3, 6, 9, and 12; and number of gadolinium-enhancing lesions on MRI at months 3, 6, 9, and 12.
